quiz_questions.question_text: Which biology career involves diagnosing and treating diseases in patients?

options.text: Physician (Doctor)
Ecologist
Agronomist
Marine biologist

quiz_questions.explanation: Physicians (MDs and DOs) diagnose and treat diseases in patients. This is one of the most well-known biology-related careers, requiring a bachelor's degree, medical school, and residency training.

quiz_questions.question_text: A forensic scientist applies biology to help solve:

options.text: Crimes — using DNA evidence, blood analysis, and toxicology
Math problems — using statistics
Engineering challenges — designing bridges
Computer bugs — debugging software

quiz_questions.explanation: Forensic scientists use biological evidence — DNA analysis, blood pattern analysis, toxicology, and trace evidence — to help solve crimes and provide evidence for legal proceedings.

quiz_questions.question_text: An epidemiologist studies:

options.text: How diseases spread through populations
How to perform surgeries
How plants grow in different soils
How to train animals

quiz_questions.explanation: Epidemiologists track how diseases spread through populations, identify risk factors, and help develop public health interventions. They played a key role during the COVID-19 pandemic.

quiz_questions.question_text: A wildlife biologist would most likely spend their time:

options.text: Tracking animal populations in natural habitats using GPS collars and field observations
Working in a hospital laboratory running blood tests
Designing artificial organs in an engineering lab
Dispensing medications at a pharmacy counter

quiz_questions.explanation: Wildlife biologists study animals in their natural environments — tracking populations, analyzing behavior, and monitoring ecological interactions through fieldwork.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which career applies biological processes to develop useful products like genetically engineered insulin or GMO crops?

options.text: Biotechnologist
Physical therapist
Nutritionist
Veterinarian

quiz_questions.explanation: Biotechnologists use living systems and biological processes to develop products and technologies — including genetically engineered medicines, GMO crops, biofuels, and diagnostic tools.

quiz_questions.question_text: An agronomist works primarily in which industry?

options.text: Agriculture — improving crops and soil management
Law enforcement — analyzing crime scene evidence
Entertainment — training animals for movies
Finance — managing investment portfolios

quiz_questions.explanation: Agronomists specialize in crop production and soil science — applying biology, chemistry, and environmental science to improve farming practices and food production.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marine biologist studies organisms that live in:

options.text: Ocean and saltwater environments
Only freshwater lakes and rivers
Outer space and other planets
Underground caves exclusively

quiz_questions.explanation: Marine biologists study life in ocean and saltwater environments — from coral reefs and deep-sea vents to coastal estuaries. They research everything from microscopic plankton to whales.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which degree is typically required to become a physician (MD)?

options.text: A bachelor's degree plus 4 years of medical school and residency
Only a high school diploma
A 2-year associate's degree
No formal education is needed

quiz_questions.explanation: Becoming a physician requires a bachelor's degree (often in biology or pre-med), then 4 years of medical school to earn an MD, followed by 3-7 years of residency training in a specialty.

quiz_questions.question_text: A genetic counselor helps patients understand:

options.text: Their genetic test results and hereditary disease risks
How to grow genetically modified crops
How to file insurance claims
How ecosystems change over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Genetic counselors help individuals understand genetic test results, assess their risk for inherited conditions, and make informed decisions about screening, treatment, or family planning.

quiz_questions.question_text: Biology careers can be broadly divided into which two main work environments?

options.text: Field-based (outdoors) and lab-based (indoors)
Desk jobs and factory jobs only
Government and private sector only
Paid and unpaid only

quiz_questions.explanation: Biology careers range from field-based work (wildlife biologist, ecologist, marine biologist tracking animals outdoors) to lab-based work (molecular biologist, lab technician running experiments indoors). Many careers involve both.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'biomedical researcher'?

options.text: A scientist who applies biology to develop medical treatments and technologies
A person who sells medical equipment to hospitals
A technician who repairs MRI machines
A historian who studies ancient medical practices

quiz_questions.explanation: Biomedical researchers use their biology expertise to investigate diseases, develop new drugs, design medical devices, and advance medical knowledge — working in universities, hospitals, and pharmaceutical companies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'epidemiologist'?

options.text: A scientist who studies the spread and control of diseases in populations
A doctor who treats skin diseases
A scientist who studies the epidermis (outer skin layer)
A statistician who only works with census data

quiz_questions.explanation: An epidemiologist studies how diseases occur and spread in populations — tracking infection rates, identifying risk factors, and recommending public health interventions. The name comes from 'epidemic,' not 'epidermis.'

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'forensic biologist'?

options.text: A scientist who uses biological evidence (DNA, blood) to help solve criminal investigations
A biologist who studies forest ecosystems
A biologist who works in foreign countries
A scientist who studies dinosaur fossils

quiz_questions.explanation: Forensic biologists apply biological techniques — DNA profiling, blood analysis, trace evidence analysis — to criminal investigations. 'Forensic' means related to courts and legal proceedings, not forests or fossils.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'biotechnologist'?

options.text: A professional who applies biological processes for industrial, medical, or agricultural purposes
A person who repairs biotechnology equipment
A teacher who lectures about technology history
A computer programmer who makes biology apps

quiz_questions.explanation: Biotechnologists use living organisms and biological systems to create useful products — genetically engineered insulin, GMO crops, biofuels, DNA testing kits, and more. They work at the intersection of biology, chemistry, and engineering.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a 'conservation biologist' focus on?

options.text: Protecting endangered species and preserving biodiversity
Conserving energy in buildings
Preserving food in canning factories
Conserving water in swimming pools

quiz_questions.explanation: Conservation biologists work to protect endangered species and preserve biodiversity — developing breeding programs, advocating for habitat protection, and researching ways to prevent species extinction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'pharmacist'?

options.text: A healthcare professional who dispenses medications and advises patients on drug interactions and proper usage
A farmer who grows pharmaceutical crops
A scientist who only does drug research in labs
A technician who manufactures pills in a factory

quiz_questions.explanation: Pharmacists are healthcare professionals who dispense prescription medications, check for drug interactions, advise patients on proper usage and side effects, and ensure medication safety. They earn a Doctor of Pharmacy (PharmD) degree.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'agronomist'?

options.text: A specialist in crop production and soil management who helps improve agricultural practices
An astronomer who studies agricultural constellations
A person who measures land plots for real estate
A chef who specializes in farm-to-table cooking

quiz_questions.explanation: Agronomists apply biology, chemistry, and environmental science to improve crop production, soil health, and farming practices. They advise farmers on crop rotation, fertilizer use, pest management, and drought resistance strategies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a 'biomedical engineer' do?

options.text: Designs medical devices and technologies by combining biology with engineering principles
Performs surgery on patients in operating rooms
Only writes biology textbooks
Manages hospital budgets and finances

quiz_questions.explanation: Biomedical engineers combine biology and engineering to design medical devices — artificial organs, prosthetics, imaging equipment (like MRI machines), and drug delivery systems. They work at the intersection of medicine and technology.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a 'physical therapist' do?

options.text: Helps patients recover from injuries and surgeries through targeted exercise and rehabilitation programs
Teaches physics classes at a university
Builds physical structures like buildings and bridges
Performs physical combat training for the military

quiz_questions.explanation: Physical therapists use their knowledge of human anatomy, physiology, and biomechanics to help patients regain strength and mobility after injuries, surgeries, or illness. They earn a Doctor of Physical Therapy (DPT) degree.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'bioinformatics'?

options.text: A field that uses computer science and statistics to analyze large biological data sets like genomes
A type of biology that studies information technology companies
A method of teaching biology using infographics
The study of biological information printed in textbooks

quiz_questions.explanation: Bioinformatics combines biology with computer science and statistics — analyzing massive data sets like genomes, protein structures, and gene expression patterns. It was essential for the Human Genome Project and continues to drive modern biology research.

quiz_questions.question_text: Maria just graduated with a biology degree and is passionate about developing new cancer treatments. She wants to work in a lab designing drugs that target specific tumor proteins. Which career path should she pursue?

options.text: Pharmaceutical researcher — she would design and test drugs targeting disease mechanisms
Epidemiologist — she would track cancer rates in populations
Wildlife biologist — she would study animals in natural habitats
Forensic scientist — she would analyze crime scene evidence

quiz_questions.explanation: Pharmaceutical researchers develop and test new drugs — designing molecules that target specific disease mechanisms is core to this career. An epidemiologist studies disease patterns in populations (not individual drug development), a wildlife biologist studies animals in natural habitats, and a forensic scientist applies biology to criminal investigations.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a murder investigation, police find a blood stain on a suspect's shirt. A specialist extracts DNA from the stain, runs it through gel electrophoresis, and compares it to the victim's DNA profile. This specialist's career is best described as:

options.text: Forensic scientist — she uses biological evidence to support criminal investigations
Genetic counselor — she interprets DNA for patients
Microbiologist — she studies microscopic organisms
Epidemiologist — she tracks disease outbreaks

quiz_questions.explanation: Forensic scientists apply biological techniques — particularly DNA analysis, blood pattern analysis, and toxicology — to help solve crimes and provide evidence for legal proceedings. A genetic counselor helps patients understand inherited disease risks, a microbiologist studies microorganisms, and an epidemiologist tracks disease spread in populations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist at Genentech uses bacteria genetically engineered to produce human insulin for diabetic patients. A student says this is 'microbiology because it involves bacteria.' Why is 'biotechnology' a MORE accurate description of this career?

options.text: The goal is to USE bacteria to CREATE a product (insulin) — applying biology for practical purposes is biotechnology
Microbiology and biotechnology are the same thing
Bacteria are not actually involved in insulin production
Biotechnology only studies viruses, not bacteria

quiz_questions.explanation: While microbiology studies bacteria broadly, biotechnology specifically applies living systems to create useful products. The key distinction is PURPOSE: microbiology aims to understand bacteria; biotechnology uses bacteria as tools to manufacture something (insulin). Before recombinant DNA technology, insulin had to be extracted from animal pancreases — this biotechnology innovation saved millions of lives.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, some scientists developed vaccines (biotechnologists), while others tracked infection rates and modeled spread (epidemiologists). A third group sequenced the virus genome to identify new variants. This third group's career is BEST described as:

options.text: Molecular biologist or bioinformatician — they sequenced and analyzed genomic data
Pharmacist — they work with medicines
Physical therapist — they help COVID patients recover
Agronomist — they study how the virus affects crops

quiz_questions.explanation: Sequencing a virus genome and identifying variants requires molecular biology and bioinformatics expertise — analyzing DNA/RNA data using specialized lab techniques and computer algorithms. This pandemic example shows how different biology careers collaborate: biotechnologists make vaccines, epidemiologists track spread, and molecular biologists characterize the pathogen itself.

quiz_questions.question_text: A college student loves both outdoor adventure and science. She wants a career where she spends most of her time in forests, wetlands, or mountains rather than in a lab. Which biology career would BEST match her preferences?

options.text: Wildlife biologist — most time is spent doing fieldwork in natural habitats
Medical lab technician — she would work in a hospital lab
Pharmacist — she would work at a pharmacy counter
Biomedical engineer — she would design devices in a lab

quiz_questions.explanation: Wildlife biologists do extensive fieldwork — tracking animals, setting camera traps, and collecting data in forests, wetlands, and mountains. Lab technicians, pharmacists, and biomedical engineers all work primarily indoors. Choosing between field-based and lab-based careers is one of the most important decisions for biology students.

quiz_questions.question_text: A young woman with a family history of breast cancer visits a specialist who analyzes her BRCA1 gene test results, explains her lifetime risk percentage, and discusses screening options like mammography. This specialist is a:

options.text: Genetic counselor — she interprets genetic test results and advises patients on hereditary risks
Oncologist — she treats existing cancers with chemotherapy
Epidemiologist — she tracks cancer rates across populations
Lab technician — she runs the genetic test machines

quiz_questions.explanation: Genetic counselors are healthcare professionals who help individuals understand genetic test results, assess hereditary disease risks, and make informed decisions about screening or treatment. An oncologist treats cancer but doesn't specialize in interpreting inheritance patterns. An epidemiologist studies disease at the population level, and a lab technician runs tests but doesn't counsel patients.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student says: 'You only need a bachelor's degree for any biology career.' A classmate disagrees. Who is correct, and why?

options.text: The classmate — many biology careers (MD, DVM, PharmD, PhD) require graduate degrees beyond a bachelor's
The first student — all biology careers start and end with a bachelor's degree
Neither — biology careers don't require any college degree
Both — it depends on whether you work in a city or rural area

quiz_questions.explanation: Different biology careers require different education levels. Some entry-level positions (lab technician, field assistant) start with a bachelor's, but physicians need an MD, vets need a DVM, pharmacists need a PharmD, physical therapists need a DPT, and research scientists often need a PhD. More patient contact and independent research generally means more education.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital lab receives a blood sample from a patient with suspected leukemia. A technician prepares microscope slides, stains white blood cells, counts cell types, and runs automated chemistry panels. She provides data to the physician but does NOT directly treat the patient. This describes a career as a:

options.text: Medical lab technician — she runs diagnostic tests that physicians use to diagnose diseases
Physician — she is the one diagnosing the patient
Pharmacist — she works with chemicals in a hospital
Genetic counselor — she analyzes the patient's cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Medical lab technicians perform diagnostic tests on biological samples — blood counts, chemistry panels, cultures, and microscopy — and provide results to physicians for diagnosis. They are essential to healthcare but typically don't interact with patients directly. A physician interprets results and treats patients; a pharmacist manages medications.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Human Genome Project (completed 2003) required geneticists to sequence DNA, computer scientists to assemble the genome, biochemists to analyze proteins, and statisticians to handle data. What does this demonstrate about modern biology careers?

options.text: Modern biology is increasingly interdisciplinary — major discoveries require collaboration across specialties
Biology careers are completely isolated from each other
Only geneticists are important in modern biology
Computer science has no place in biology careers

quiz_questions.explanation: The Human Genome Project is a landmark example of interdisciplinary biology — it required geneticists, bioinformaticians, biochemists, and statisticians working together across international teams. Modern biology careers increasingly blend traditional biology with technology, data science, and engineering in fields like computational biology, systems biology, and synthetic biology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students debate biology careers. Student A says: 'Veterinarians are basically the same as human doctors — just for animals.' Student B says: 'No, vets are more like zoologists.' Which statement is MORE accurate, and why?

options.text: Student A is closer — both vets and MDs diagnose and treat diseases, but vets must know anatomy across MANY species
Student B is correct — veterinarians only study animals, not treat them
Neither — veterinarians only work with dogs and cats
Both are wrong — veterinarians are a type of ecologist

quiz_questions.explanation: Veterinarians are most similar to human physicians — both diagnose diseases, prescribe treatments, and perform surgeries. The key difference is that vets must understand anatomy and disease across many species (mammals, birds, reptiles, fish), not just one. Zoologists study animals scientifically but don't treat their diseases. Vets earn a DVM degree through 4 years of veterinary school, similar to an MD's training path.
